misconceptions.correction: Similar: same angles, proportional sides. Congruent is the special case where ratio = 1.

misconceptions.misconception: AA similarity and congruence are different; don't see how similarity is generalization of congruence
misconceptions.correction: AA proves similarity. If angles match, sides must be proportional. Congruence is when ratio = 1.

misconceptions.misconception: Special right triangles (30-60-90, 45-45-90) have side ratios that must be memorized
misconceptions.correction: Ratios derive from Pythagorean theorem. 45-45-90: legs equal → hyp = leg√2. 30-60-90: use Pythagorean theorem.

essentialUnderstandings: Similar triangles have equal angles and proportional corresponding sides
AA, SAS, and SSS similarity criteria establish when triangles are similar
Special right triangles have predictable side ratios enabling quick solutions

misconceptions.misconception: Central angle and arc measure are related only by memorized rule; unclear why equal
misconceptions.correction: Central angle and its intercepted arc have equal measures by definition of arc measure.

misconceptions.misconception: Inscribed angle is 'any angle inside circle'; misses vertex-on-circle requirement
misconceptions.correction: Inscribed angle: vertex on circle, sides are chords. Measure is half the intercepted arc.

misconceptions.misconception: Tangent-radius relationship is rule to apply; why perpendicular isn't explained
misconceptions.correction: Tangent touches circle at one point. If not perpendicular to radius, another point on tangent would be inside circle (contradiction).

essentialUnderstandings: Central angle measure equals intercepted arc measure by definition
Inscribed angle measure is half its intercepted arc (angle inscribed in semicircle is right angle)
Tangent line is perpendicular to radius at point of tangency

misconceptions.misconception: Pyramid and cone volumes are half of prism/cylinder by rule; reasoning unclear
misconceptions.correction: Both are 1/3 of prism/cylinder with same base and height. Derivation uses limits or decomposition.

misconceptions.misconception: Surface area and volume are similar concepts; both measure 'size'
misconceptions.correction: Surface area is outer boundary (square units). Volume is interior space (cubic units). Different dimensions.

misconceptions.misconception: All pyramids with same base area and height have same volume regardless of base shape
misconceptions.correction: True. V = 1/3 × base area × height. Base shape doesn't matter, only its area.

essentialUnderstandings: Volume formulas relate by a factor of 1/3 for cones/pyramids vs cylinders/prisms
Surface area is sum of all face areas; volume is three-dimensional interior space
Scaling dimensions by factor k multiplies volume by k³

misconceptions.misconception: Distance formula comes from nowhere; arbitrary algebraic manipulation
misconceptions.correction: Distance formula derives from Pythagorean theorem applied to horizontal/vertical distances.

misconceptions.misconception: Proving quadrilateral is rectangle requires checking all four angles are 90°
misconceptions.correction: Sufficient: show opposite sides equal in length, OR opposite sides parallel and one angle is 90°.

misconceptions.misconception: Proofs in coordinate geometry are tedious; no advantage over other methods
misconceptions.correction: Coordinate geometry makes some proofs systematic (e.g., showing congruence via distance formula).

essentialUnderstandings: Distance formula d = √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²] extends Pythagorean theorem to coordinate plane
Slope determines parallelism (equal slopes) and perpendicularity (negative reciprocals)
Coordinate proofs use algebra and geometry systematically to verify properties

misconceptions.misconception: Complex numbers i² = -1 are 'imaginary' and not real/useful
misconceptions.correction: Complex numbers extend reals; essential in engineering, physics, and mathematics for completeness.

misconceptions.misconception: Vertex form f(x) = a(x-h)² + k has vertex at (h, k) with no understanding of meaning
misconceptions.correction: Vertex (h, k) is minimum/maximum. Parameter a affects direction (up/down) and width.

misconceptions.misconception: Discriminant b² - 4ac only determines 'if solutions exist' (real or complex)
misconceptions.correction: Discriminant also determines solution type: two distinct (>0), one repeated (=0), or two complex (<0).

essentialUnderstandings: Complex numbers a + bi extend reals to solve equations like x² = -1
Vertex form reveals vertex location and transformations; standard form shows x-intercepts
Discriminant determines nature and quantity of solutions

misconceptions.misconception: End behavior depends on individual terms; degree and leading coefficient matter separately
misconceptions.correction: End behavior determined by leading term (highest degree, its coefficient). Other terms irrelevant for limits.

misconceptions.misconception: Rational Root Theorem requires testing every possible root manually
misconceptions.correction: Theorem lists candidates. Use synthetic division or substitution to test efficiently. Narrows search significantly.

misconceptions.misconception: Multiplicity of roots is 'how many times root appears'; impact on graph unclear
misconceptions.correction: Multiplicity m > 1: graph touches but doesn't cross at that root. Odd multiplicity: crosses. Even multiplicity: touches.

essentialUnderstandings: End behavior is determined by degree (odd/even) and leading coefficient sign
Rational Root Theorem provides candidates for rational roots; test efficiently
Multiplicity affects graph behavior at roots: touches (even) or crosses (odd)

misconceptions.misconception: Exponential growth vs polynomial growth: both 'get big,' hard to distinguish
misconceptions.correction: Exponential eventually dominates any polynomial. 2ˣ > x¹⁰ for large x. Exponential is much faster.

misconceptions.misconception: Logarithms are arbitrary notation logₐ(b); meaning as 'inverse exponential' unclear
misconceptions.correction: log_a(b) = c means aᶜ = b. Logarithm is the exponent; inverse of exponential.

misconceptions.misconception: Logarithm properties (product, quotient, power) are rules to memorize
misconceptions.correction: Properties follow from exponential properties. log(xy) = log(x) + log(y) because aᵐ · aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ.

essentialUnderstandings: Exponential functions (bases > 1) grow faster than polynomial functions
Logarithm is the inverse of exponential; logₐ(b) is the exponent to which a must be raised to get b
Logarithm properties follow from properties of exponents

misconceptions.misconception: Simplifying rational expressions: canceling factors happens arbitrarily without care
misconceptions.correction: Can only cancel common factors. (x+3)/(x²-9) = (x+3)/((x-3)(x+3)) = 1/(x-3), noting x ≠ 3.

misconceptions.misconception: Vertical asymptotes are 'forbidden x-values'; removed discontinuities don't cause asymptotes
misconceptions.correction: Vertical asymptote: denominator zero after simplification. Removable discontinuity (hole): factor cancels.

misconceptions.misconception: Solving rational equations: multiply through by LCD, then solve; extraneous solutions overlooked
misconceptions.correction: After solving, check solutions don't make original denominator zero. Extraneous solutions must be excluded.

essentialUnderstandings: Rational expressions are simplified by factoring and canceling common factors
Vertical asymptotes occur where denominator is zero; removable discontinuities have canceling factors
Solving rational equations requires checking solutions are valid (don't make denominator zero)

misconceptions.misconception: Arithmetic and geometric sequences are unrelated; memorize both formulas separately
misconceptions.correction: Arithmetic: constant common difference. Geometric: constant common ratio. Both are special functions of n.

misconceptions.misconception: Infinite geometric series never converge; sum doesn't exist
misconceptions.correction: Geometric series converges if |r| < 1. S = a₁/(1-r). Diverges if |r| ≥ 1.

misconceptions.misconception: Summation notation Σ is just shorthand; doesn't represent an operation with meaning
misconceptions.correction: Summation is a operation: Σ(n=1 to 5) n² means 1² + 2² + 3² + 4² + 5² = 55.

essentialUnderstandings: Arithmetic sequences have constant differences; explicit formula aₙ = a₁ + (n-1)d
Geometric sequences have constant ratios; explicit formula aₙ = a₁ · rⁿ⁻¹
Infinite geometric series converge when |r| < 1 with sum S = a₁/(1-r)

misconceptions.misconception: Trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) only apply to right triangles
misconceptions.correction: Trig ratios are defined via unit circle for any angle, including obtuse and negative angles.

misconceptions.misconception: sin(x) and sin⁻¹(x) are reciprocals; sin⁻¹ means 1/sin(x)
misconceptions.correction: sin⁻¹(x) is inverse function (arcsin), not reciprocal. Reciprocal of sin(x) is csc(x) = 1/sin(x).

misconceptions.misconception: All angles have unique trigonometric values; no periodicity awareness
misconceptions.correction: Trig functions are periodic. sin(θ + 360°) = sin(θ). Multiple angles have same trig value.

essentialUnderstandings: Trigonometric functions extend beyond right triangles via unit circle definitions
Sine and cosine are periodic with period 2π; tangent period is π
Inverse trig functions find angles from ratio values; different from reciprocals

misconceptions.misconception: Identities are formulas to memorize; don't see derivation or connections
misconceptions.correction: Identities follow from Pythagorean theorem and angle addition. Understanding derivation aids memory.

misconceptions.misconception: Solving trig equations: use formula, get answer, done (extraneous solutions missed)
misconceptions.correction: Trig equations have infinitely many solutions (periodicity). In [0, 2π), find all. Check extraneous.

misconceptions.misconception: Sum and difference formulas are arbitrary rules without geometric meaning
misconceptions.correction: sin(A+B) = sin(A)cos(B) + cos(A)sin(B) comes from rotating angle on unit circle and projecting.

essentialUnderstandings: Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 relates sine and cosine
Angle sum/difference formulas enable evaluation at non-standard angles
Solving trig equations yields infinite solution sets; consider periodicity

misconceptions.misconception: lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a) always; limit and function value are same thing
misconceptions.correction: Limit is value f(x) approaches as x approaches a. f(a) is the function value. Often equal, but not always.

misconceptions.misconception: Limit notation lim(x→∞) f(x) means 'x becomes infinitely large'
misconceptions.correction: x approaches ∞ without reaching it. Limit describes f(x) behavior as x grows without bound.

misconceptions.misconception: Function is continuous if graphable without lifting pencil; formal definition unclear
misconceptions.correction: Continuity at a: lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a). Three conditions: limit exists, function value exists, they're equal.

essentialUnderstandings: Limit describes function behavior as input approaches a value (without necessarily reaching it)
Continuity at a point requires: limit exists, function value exists, and they're equal
Removable discontinuities (holes) vs non-removable (jumps, vertical asymptotes)

misconceptions.misconception: Combinations and permutations are similar; order doesn't matter in either
misconceptions.correction: Permutations: order matters (arrangements). Combinations: order doesn't (selections). P(n,r) ≠ C(n,r) unless r = 1.

misconceptions.misconception: Probability of event and odds are the same thing
misconceptions.correction: Probability: P(E) = favorable/total. Odds: favorable:unfavorable. Related but different.

misconceptions.misconception: Binomial probability applies to all repeated trials
misconceptions.correction: Binomial requires: fixed n, two outcomes, constant p, independent trials. Not all repeated situations qualify.

essentialUnderstandings: Permutations count ordered arrangements; combinations count unordered selections
Binomial probability: P(X = k) = C(n,k) p^k (1-p)^(n-k) for fixed n, p
Probability and odds are related but distinct measures of likelihood

misconceptions.misconception: Confidence interval like [50, 60] means 'true parameter has 95% chance of being in this interval'
misconceptions.correction: CI is fixed after computing. Parameter is fixed. Correct: '95% of repeated samples give CIs containing parameter.'

misconceptions.misconception: P-value is probability that null hypothesis is true
misconceptions.correction: P-value is probability of observing data (or more extreme) IF null is true. Different from P(H₀ true).

misconceptions.misconception: Type I and Type II errors are equally important; minimize both equally
misconceptions.correction: Context determines which is worse. Significance level α controls Type I error.

essentialUnderstandings: Confidence intervals estimate population parameters with a margin of error
P-value is conditional probability of data given null hypothesis
Type I error (rejecting true null) and Type II error (failing to reject false null) have different implications

misconceptions.misconception: Students think the longer object is always heavier
misconceptions.correction: Length and weight are different properties. A long feather is lighter than a short rock.
misconceptions.diagnostic: Give the student a long straw and a short block. Ask which is heavier and why.

misconceptions.misconception: Students only compare objects placed side-by-side with aligned endpoints
misconceptions.correction: Objects can be compared even when endpoints are not aligned, by marking and measuring.
misconceptions.diagnostic: Place two objects with staggered starting points. Ask which is longer.

essentialUnderstandings: Objects have measurable attributes like length, weight, and capacity.
Direct comparison requires the same attribute (compare length to length, not length to weight).
Measurement involves iterating a unit and counting.

misconceptions.misconception: Students sort by only one attribute and cannot re-sort by a different rule
misconceptions.correction: The same set of objects can be sorted multiple ways depending on the chosen attribute.
misconceptions.diagnostic: Give the student a mixed set of objects. After they sort by color, ask them to sort the same objects by size.

misconceptions.misconception: Students think sorting categories must be equal in size
misconceptions.correction: When sorting real data, groups often have different counts. Unequal groups are normal.
misconceptions.diagnostic: Give 3 red, 5 blue, and 1 yellow block. Ask the student to sort and count each group.

essentialUnderstandings: Objects can be classified and sorted by measurable attributes (color, shape, size, type).
The same collection can be sorted different ways using different rules.
Data can be organized and counted to answer questions.
